coredata.dc:title: Novel Enteromorpha Prolifera based carbon dots: Probing the radical scavenging of natural phenolic compounds
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaojuan
Chen, Qi
Zhang, Zhengyuan
He, Hua
Ma, Xiqi
Liu, Zhenzhen
Ge, Baosheng
Huang, Fang
coredata.dc:description: Phenolic compounds can act as protective agents against free radical oxidation. It is desirable to develop simple and efficient analytical techniques to measure the phenolic content and their antioxidant capacity. Using Enteromorpha prolifera (EP) as the carbon source, novel fluorescent carbon dots (EP-CDs) were synthesized through a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. EP-CDs exhibit high mono-dispersity in aqueous solution, low cytotoxicity, and dual fluorescence emission in the visible and near-infrared range. The fluorescence of EP-CDs is inert to phenolic compounds and hydroxyl free radicals, but could be rapidly and efficiently quenched by the quinoid oxidation products. By monitoring the fluorescence quenching of EP-CDs, it is demonstrated that the radical scavenging processes of different natural phenolic compounds could be sensitively detected. By employing this eco-friendly and economic analytical method, phenols with different structure or concentration could be rapidly detected and determined. EP-CDs also have potential for additional applications, such as hydrogen peroxide and iron analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Carbon dots
Phenolic compounds
Radicals
Green tides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots using Enteromorpha prolifera as raw materials
Probing radical scavenging of natural phenolic compounds

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Qingdao Municipal People's Livelihood Science and Technology Project
Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 21573289 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (grant no. ZR2017MB039 ), the Key Technologies R&D Program of Shandong Province (grant no. 2018GGX102025 , 2018GSF121039 ), and the Qingdao Municipal People's Livelihood Science and Technology Project (grant no. 17-3-3-76-nsh ). The authors gratefully thank Prof. Dr. Werner M. Nau for his useful suggestions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of EP-CDs and the fluorescence quenching of phenolic compounds by the oxidation products.
Characterization of EP-CDs. (A) HRTEM images of EP-CDs. (B) Histogram depicting the size distribution of EP-CDs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of EP-CDs. (D) XRD spectrum of EP-CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum, (B) N 1s, (C) C 1s, and (D) O 1s spectra of EP-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum (λ ex = 380 nm) of EP-CDs. (B) Photoluminescence emission spectra of a dilute aqueous solution of EP-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (2D representation).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of EP-CDs (25.0 μg mL−1) in the absence and presence of different additives, namely chlorogenic acid (CA, 1.0 mM), HRP (5.0 μg mL−1), and H2O2 (10.0 mM). (B)Kinetic trace of EP-CDs fluorescence quenching (λ ex = 380 nm, λ em = 454 nm) after mixing with CA, HRP, and H2O2 (same concentrations as in A).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of EP-CDs after mixing with different phenolic compounds at the same concentration (1.0 mM) in the presence of HRP (5.0 μg mL−1) and H2O2 (10.0 mM). (B) Photographs of the mixtures under daylight and illuminated by a UV lamp (365 nm).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of EP-CDs (25.0 μg mL−1) after mixing with catechin at different concentrations in the presence of HRP (5.0 μg mL−1) and H2O2 (10.0 mM). (B) Linear relationship of the fluorescence intensity change (F 0/F) versus concentration of catechin (the inset shows photographs of the mixtures under daylight and under a 365 nm UV lamp).

head.ce:title: Novel Enteromorpha Prolifera based carbon dots: Probing the radical scavenging of natural phenolic compounds

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing and Centre for Bioengineering & Biotechnology, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing and Centre for Bioengineering & Biotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phenolic compounds can act as protective agents against free radical oxidation. It is desirable to develop simple and efficient analytical techniques to measure the phenolic content and their antioxidant capacity. Using Enteromorpha prolifera (EP) as the carbon source, novel fluorescent carbon dots (EP-CDs) were synthesized through a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. EP-CDs exhibit high mono-dispersity in aqueous solution, low cytotoxicity, and dual fluorescence emission in the visible and near-infrared range. The fluorescence of EP-CDs is inert to phenolic compounds and hydroxyl free radicals, but could be rapidly and efficiently quenched by the quinoid oxidation products. By monitoring the fluorescence quenching of EP-CDs, it is demonstrated that the radical scavenging processes of different natural phenolic compounds could be sensitively detected. By employing this eco-friendly and economic analytical method, phenols with different structure or concentration could be rapidly detected and determined. EP-CDs also have potential for additional applications, such as hydrogen peroxide and iron analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Free radicals and reactive oxygen species (ROS) are harmful compounds, which are involved in metabolic, neurodegenerative, cardiovascular as well as mitochondrial diseases and also cancer [1,2]. Antioxidants have proven highly effective in inhibiting the radical-chain oxidation by free radicals and ROS. They could consequently be vital for therapy and the prevention of the associated disorders [3,4]. Phenolic compounds are natural antioxidants abundantly found in a large variety of foods, such as vegetables, fruits, spices, herbs, grains, oils, and teas. Their antioxidant activity is mainly attributed to their roles as reducing agents, hydrogen donors, and singlet oxygen quenchers [5,6]. Therefore, the consumption of food with high phenolic content is an irrevocable trend.

The phenolic content and the antioxidant capacity of foods and natural products are important properties for the evaluation of their quality attributes. In general, spectrophotometric methods, including DPPH, ABTS, and Folin-Ciocalteu assays that employ different chromogenic redox reagents, have been mostly used to measure the total antioxidant capacity [7–11]. Alternative, electroanalytical and some fluorescent methods have also emerged [12–15]. In recent years, more advanced and powerful techniques, such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), mass spectrometry (MS), and gas chromatography coupled mass spectrometry (GC–MS), have been applied to the accurate characterization of phenolic compounds [16–19]. Nevertheless, the design of new systems with simple and efficient analytical techniques presents an important field of developmental research.

In this study, we report a novel fluorescent assay for probing the radical scavenging of natural phenolic compounds, which employs carbon dots synthesized from Enteromorpha prolifera (EP-CDs) as the fluorescent reagent. Carbon dots (CDs), which normally refer to carbon-based nanomaterials between 1 to 20 nm in size, are relatively young members among photoluminescent materials. Relying on their ultra-small size and intrinsic fluorescence, many potential applications in sensing and imaging have been proposed [20]. Besides standard laboratory chemicals, such as citric acid, ethylenediamine, cysteine, poly-ethylenimine, and polyacrylamide [21–24], thousands of natural substances, including prawn shells, egg white, silkworm chrysalis, cabbage, and lychee seeds, have been used as raw materials for the synthesis of CDs [25–35]. Enteromorpha prolifera is a kind of green marine algae growing in offshore shoals. In recent years, global warming and water eutrophication have been leading to a large-scale bloom of Enteromorpha prolifera, among others in China, that, known as green tides, has a significant negative impact on the coastal environment and aquaculture [36,37]. Here, we demonstrate that Enteromorpha prolifera could be turned into a valuable resource by using this waste material as a new carbon source for the green synthesis of fluorescent CDs. Furthermore, EP-CDs are demonstrated to serve as fluorescent sensors for probing the radical scavenging of natural phenolic compounds.

1,4-Benzoquinone and catechuic acid were obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. 3,4-Dihydroxycinnamic acid (caffeic acid), 1,3,4,5-tetrahydroxycyclohexane-carboxylic acid (chlorogenic acid), horse radish peroxidase (HRP), and 3-hydroxytyramine hydrochloride (dopamine) were from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Hydrogen peroxide was produced by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ) purified on a Millipore system (Millipore, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots using Enteromorpha prolifera as raw materials
ce:section.ce:para: Enteromorpha prolifera was collected from the coastal beach in Qingdao, Shandong. It was soaked and washed first with tap water and subsequently ultrapure water. After draining well, 150 g of Enteromorpha prolifera and 600 g of ultrapure water were placed into a domestic food processor mixer for 15 min. The resulting liquid stock was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 15 min, 15℃, Eppendorf Centrifuge 5810R) to collect Enteromorpha prolifera as a green paste. 10 g of the paste was added into a sealed hydrothermal vessel and reacted in a microwave digestion furnace at 200 °C for 30 min (30 atm, 500 W, WX-4000 Microwave Digestion System). After the reaction mixture was allowed to cool to ambient temperature, the obtained dark brown solution was filtered through a microporous filtering film (0.22 μm, Shanghai Xingya Purifying Co.) to remove large aggregates. The colloidal solution was repeatedly dialyzed against ultrapure water in a dialysis bag (retaining molecular weight 1000 D, Amicon Ultra, Millipore) for 24 h to obtain a pure EP-CDs solution.

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Steady-state fluorescence spectra were collected on a FluoroMax-4 fluorometer (Hitachi Limited, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Hitachi Limited, Japan). High-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were measured on a JEM-2100 electron microscope at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). An A-mode X-ray diffraction system (XRD, Rigaku Corporation, Japan) was used to check the structure and an Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific, America) was used to carry out elemental and functional group analysis.

Tris−HCl buffer solution (20.0 mM, pH = 7.38) was used to prepare stock solutions of EP-CDs (1.0 mg mL−1), H2O2 (100.0 mM), HRP (100.0 μg mL−1), and phenolic compounds (1.0 mM). Working solutions were prepared by diluting aliquots of stock solutions with Tris−HCl buffer. The final concentrations of EP-CDs, H2O2, and HRP were kept as 25.0 μg mL−1, 10.0 mM, and 5.0 μg mL−1, respectively. Steady-state fluorescence spectra of EP-CDs were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm after 10 min of mixing. For the kinetic experiments, the fluorescence intensity of EP-CDs at 454 nm was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm after a varied period of mixing.

HeLa cells were incubated in a DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum in a humidified incubator at 37 °C in which the CO2 level was kept constant at 5%. The in vitro cytotoxicity of EP-CDs was evaluated through the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) cell viability assay. The cells were harvested from 90% confluent cell culture plates and resuspended in fresh complete medium. The cells (1 × 104) were seeded into 96-well plates and cultured for 24 h at 37 °C in an atmosphere of humidified 5% CO2. The supernatant was replaced by EP-CDs solution at different concentrations, while the control group was replaced by fresh culture medium. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, each well was washed twice with PBS and 100 μL of medium containing MTT (0.5 mg mL−1) was added into each well before incubation for another 4 h. Then the medium was removed, another 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added and incubated for 15 min. Finally, the optical absorbance at 570 nm was measured by using a microplate autoreader (SpectraMak Mze) and the cell viability ratio was calculated using A 570/A 0,570.

Enteromorpha prolifera is the major constituent of green tides, which has a major negative ecological and economic impact worldwide. This green, filamentous, and branched macroalga has hollow thalli with cells containing starch grains, large parietal chloroplasts, and vacuoles that reach up to 40% of cell volume [37]. In this study, Enteromorpha prolifera is used as raw material for the synthesis of fluorescent CDs (EP-CDs, Scheme 1 ) to turn waste into valuable nano-probes. It is worth noting that in our method the synthetic reaction was conducted in a sealed vessel placed in a microwave digestion furnace at controlled temperature and pressure. Upon microwave irradiation, the temperature of the reaction solution could be rapidly elevated to 200 °C, and product yields reached 0.75 ± 0.25%. Compared to traditional hydrothermal methods, which usually require 2–8 h, several steps and strict conditions to complete the reaction, we were able to accomplish this one-step synthesis process in 30 min without need for extra additives. This should allow scalability of the synthesis, energy-saving, and shorter reaction times [22,25–33,38].

The EP-CDs products were characterized by HRTEM on different scales. As shown in Fig. 1 A, most EP-CDs are spherical particles with a diameter ranging from 2 to 6 nm and well dispersed on the surface of Cu grids. The corresponding size distribution is plotted in Fig. 1B, from which an average diameter of 3.6 nm is obtained, similar to that of previously described CDs [21,28,32,39–41].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectric spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out to characterize the functional groups of EP-CDs. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1C), the two peaks near 3350 cm−1 and the peak at 2940 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, NH and CH groups. The characteristic bands at 1770 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 belong to CO stretching vibrations in carboxyl groups and CC stretching bands, respectively. Peaks near 1400 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1 are assigned to CN and CO stretching bands [25,32,42,43]. A typical XRD pattern in Fig. 1D presents one broad peak at about 2θ = 23.0°, which is ascribed to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon [44,45]. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2 A) shows three obvious peaks corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s. Additionally, due to the biological complexity of Enteromorpha prolifera, signals from other atoms, such as Ca, are also detected. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) exhibits two main peaks at 399.7 and 400.3 eV, which are ascribed to CN and NH, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) demonstrates the presence of CC/CC bond with a binding energy at 284.6 eV. The remaining three deconvoluted peaks are corresponding to CN, CO, and CO at 285.5, 286.3, and 287.9 eV. Deconvolution of the O 1 s peak (Fiure. 2D) shows two components at 531.6 and 532.8 eV for CO and CO, respectively [22,32–34,41]. These XPS results coincide with the FT-IR analysis, indicating the existence of both, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of EP-CDs.

The UV–vis spectrum of EP-CDs (Fig. 3 A) shows an absorption peak at 282 nm, attributable to a π–π* transition. When excited at 380 nm, a bright blue fluorescence emission at 450 nm is observed (Fig. 3A). In addition, EP-CDs exhibit a relative weak but clear-near infrared fluorescence (NIR) peak around 845 nm. Consistent with the previously reported carbon dots, EP-CDs also exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence emission (Fig. 3B). As the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 to 500 nm, the FL peak of EP-CDs shifts toward longer wavelengths, from 410 to 545 nm. Correspondingly, the NIR emission peak also shows a bathochromic shift. This property opens the possibility for multichannel analysis with EP-CDs.

Further experiments revealed that the fluorescence of EP-CDs is pH dependent and can be effectively quenched by Fe3+ ions. As shown in Figures S1A and B, the fluorescence intensity of EP-CDs only changes slightly in the solutions with pH values in the physiological range, but decreases significantly when the pH value is very low (<4) or high (>8). When EP-CDs were mixed with solutions containing a variety of metal ions at a relatively high concentration (20.0 mM), amoung others Ag+, K+, Li+, Na+, NH4 +, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ce2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, In3+, La3+, and Zr4+, the fluorescence of EP-CDs was selectively quenched by Fe3+ with high specificity. Although the mechanism of the photoluminescence and quenching of EP-CDs has not yet accurately understood, our exploratory results suggest that EP-CDs could serve as promising Fe3+ sensors, similar to other CDs.

An MTT assay was carried out to evaluate the biocompatibility of EP-CDs. As shown in Figure S1C, after incubation with highly concentrated EP-CDs (up to 1.0 mg mL−1) for 24 h, HeLa cells maintained above 85% viability, indicating a low cytotoxicity of EP-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing radical scavenging of natural phenolic compounds
ce:section.ce:para: EP-CDs can be synthesized from Enteromorpha prolifera waste by a facile procedure and applied as eco-friendly multicolor probes [32,38]. It has been reported that the fluorescence of CDs is strongly dependent upon the nature of their surfaces. When certain molecules interact with CDs, significant fluorescence quenching or enhancement has been observed [46–51]. Herein, we demonstrate that EP-CDs are effectively quenched by electron-acceptor compounds, specifically quinones. More important, EP-CDs are inert to the phenolic precursors, which, however, can be oxidized to the quinone structures in the presence of free radicals. Therefore, EP-CDs could be employed as fluorescent probes to detect the radical scavenging activity of natural phenolic compounds and to measure the antioxidant capacity of phenolic solutions and/or extracts.

The quenching effect of quinones on the fluorescence of EP-CDs was investigated first. 1,4-Benzoquinone was chosen as a model compound. As shown in Figure S2, the fluorescence of EP-CDs responds promptly after mixing with 1,4-benzoquinone (1.0 mM) and the intensity decreased dramatically within 10 min. The quenching effect becomes more significant as the concentration of 1,4-benzoquinone is increased. 1,4-Benzoquinone is a well-known electron acceptor, which presumably affects static or dynamic quenching of EP-CDs by an electron transfer process [46].

We chose a natural phenolic compound, 1,3,4,5-tetrahydroxycyclohexane-carboxylic acid (chlorogenic acid, CA) to demonstrate the idea of using EP-CDs to probe the radical scavenging propensity of phenols. Horse radish peroxidase (HRP, 5.0 μg mL−1) and excess H2O2 (10.0 mM) were employed to produce free radicals to be scavenged by CA (1.0 mM). Ideally, the resulting quinone-structured products should be proportional to the amount of CA and measurable by a varying quenching effect of EP-CDs.

A series of control experiments were carried out to check the quenching effects of the catalyst and reactants. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, when EP-CDs (25 μg mL−1) are only mixed with a single component either CA (1.0 mM), HRP (5.0 μg mL−1), or H2O2 (10.0 mM), the fluorescence change was insignificant. In addition, EP-CDs were also inert to the highly concentrated free radicals produced by the HRP-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2. Only when the catalyst and all reactants were present, i.e., only when the quinone-structured products are obtained in the mixture, the fluorescence of EP-CDs dropped sharply (by 78% for [CA] = 1.0 mM). Fig. 4B indicates that the fluorescence of EP-CDs is quenched very rapidly and that the reaction is complete after 2 min. These results show that the fluorescence of EP-CDs is efficiently and specifically quenched by the quinone compounds produced by the radical-induced oxidation of CA.

In order to verify the general utility of this sensing method, three other natural phenolic compounds, catechin (catechuic acid), caffeic acid (3,4-dihydroxycinnamic acid), and dopamine (3-hydroxytyramine hydrochloride) were investigated. The experimental results show that the fluorescence of EP-CDs does not afford a significant change after mixing with any one of the three phenolic compounds alone. Only when free radicals were produced (through HRP-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2), the fluorescence of EP-CDs starts to decrease rapidly in all three cases (Figure S3). Furthermore, radical scavenging by dopamine was less effective (slower) than that for catechin and caffeic acid, but all reactions were complete after 10 min. In the presence of excess free radicals (ROS), the reaction products of different phenols (at the same concentration of 1.0 mM, mixing for 10 min) exhibit different quenching effects (Fig. 5 ). Catechin stands out, with an extremely strong quenching ability of its oxidation products, which might be due to its specific flavonoid structure. Similar results have been reported in previously published comparative studies [52,53]. It is suggested that the 5,7-dihydroxyphenolic arrangement in the A ring of catechin contributes markedly to its antioxidation activity. It was noted that the fluorescence emission spectra shifted slightly upon quenching, which is related to the intrinsic weak fluorescence properties of the oxidation products.

We also determined the fluorescence quantum yields of EP-CDs before and after quenching according to the methods of Williams and Ci et al. [54,55]. The quantum yield (QY) of as-synthesized EP-CDs was found to be 14.0 ± 0.2%, using quinine sulfate as the reference standard. However, after quenching by quinoid reaction products of different phenols, the QY of EP-CDs dropped remarkably (Fig. 5). The most dramatic decrease was found for catechin (QY was reduced to 2.2%), while the oxidation products of CA, caffeic acid, and dopamine were able to bring down the QY of EP-CDs to 4–5%.

Keeping HRP and H2O2 at the same concentration to produce excess radicals and fixing the reaction time (10 min), the fluorescence intensity of EP-CDs decreases gradually as the concentration of catechin increases (Fig. 6 ). A good linear relationship is obtained between the fluorescence quenching effect (F 0/F) and the catechin concentration over the range from 0 to 80 μM (y = 12.65x + 1.14, R 2 = 0.998), which should allow quantification. The detection limit for catechin was found to be 46 nM at a signal to noise ratio of 3.

Employing a facile microwave-assisted procedure, EP-CDs were obtained as novel fluorescent probes. The fluorescence of EP-CDs is inert to phenolic compounds and hydroxyl free radicals but could be rapidly and efficiently quenched by quinone-based products. This property can be exploited to probe the radical-scavenging processes of different natural phenolic compounds. The reaction can be monitored through the fluorescence quenching of EP-CDs. With excess hydroxyl free radicals, phenols with different structure can be differentiated and the phenol content of solutions can be determined by a linear quenching dependence. The novel probes offer a method to detect phenols and to determine the antioxidant capacity of foods and natural products. On the basis of these findings, EP-CDs have additional application potential for hydrogen peroxide sensing. Experiments in this direction are underway.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 21573289), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (grant no. ZR2017MB039), the Key Technologies R&D Program of Shandong Province (grant no. 2018GGX102025, 2018GSF121039), and the Qingdao Municipal People's Livelihood Science and Technology Project (grant no. 17-3-3-76-nsh). The authors gratefully thank Prof. Dr. Werner M. Nau for his useful suggestions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.11.019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
